messages.content: [Plan Importer · Roundtable] How does this plan fit our Catholic family rhythms and faith life?

**Lorenzo:** This plan beautifully centers food as an act of love and celebration for JP's sacrament — the jambalaya made the night before, the custom cake with its faith inscription, the thoughtful cheese and fruit spreads all create a feast worthy of this milestone. I'd suggest we think about the meal not just as party food but as a true reception table where guests can linger and celebrate what Confirmation means. Perhaps we set the cake in a place of honor with JP's candle from the Mass nearby, making the reveal moment feel sacred as well as celebratory.

**Coach:** The week leading up to Confirmation is packed with indoor tasks — cleaning, shopping, prep — so I want to make sure the boys, especially Joseph and Michael, get outside time to burn energy and stay regulated. I'd recommend we protect a few outdoor windows: maybe Joseph helps inflate and test the bounce house on May 10 as active play, and the morning of May 16 we do a short family walk before getting dressed. This keeps everyone grounded in their bodies so they can be fully present for the Mass and celebration.

**Dr. Monica:** For James at 13 months, a 1:30 PM Mass lands right in typical nap territory, so Lauren should plan for him to nap earlier that morning or accept he may be drowsy or fussy during the service — having a quiet exit strategy is wise. Michael at 5 can participate meaningfully if we prepare him ahead of time: explain what Confirmation is in simple terms, maybe read a picture book about the Holy Spirit, so he feels included rather than just told to sit still. Both little ones will do better at the reception if the bounce house is ready as an immediate outlet after the more formal church time.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** Lauren, this plan is already beautifully ordered around what matters most — JP's step deeper into his faith, witnessed and celebrated by the people who love him. I'd encourage you to add a few intentional faith touches: perhaps a family prayer the night before as you finish the jambalaya together, asking the Holy Spirit to prepare JP's heart. On the morning of May 16, before the rush of getting dressed, gather everyone briefly to pray over JP — this grounds the whole day in its true purpose. Consider placing his Confirmation candle on the food table near the cake, creating a visual reminder that this feast flows from the altar. The gift reveal during cake time is a perfect moment to speak words of blessing over him — let John or both of you share what you hope for his life in the Spirit. Everything else — the clean house, the food, the bounce house for the little ones — serves this sacred center. You've built a plan that honors both the sacrament and your family's real life; trust it, and let the Holy Spirit do the rest.
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Father Gregory:** The plan wisely suggests JP could help with construction, but I'd make that concrete now rather than leaving it as a vague "consider" item. This is a perfect applied science opportunity—soil chemistry, plant biology, even geometry for the bed dimensions. I'd also suggest having Joseph document the project with a simple garden journal; tracking growth over the summer makes excellent nature study.

**Lorenzo:** From a kitchen perspective, I'd want to know *which varieties* you're planting—a Roma tomato serves very differently than a Cherokee Purple, and pickling cucumbers versus slicers changes how I'd plan summer meals. Also, consider adding one basil plant near the tomatoes; they're companion plants and you'll want fresh basil when those tomatoes come in. Don't skip the fish emulsion step—it really does make a difference for transplant survival.

**Coach:** The physical work today is real—hauling compost bags, laying and wetting cardboard. With James at 13 months and Michael at 5, Lauren needs a plan for *when* she'll do this. I'd suggest late afternoon when John's home, or build it into outdoor time where the boys can dig in nearby dirt while she works. Don't underestimate the bending and lifting; pace yourself and hydrate.

**Dr. Monica:** Keep Michael involved but set clear boundaries around the blood meal and bone meal—those amendments aren't toxic in small amounts but shouldn't be handled or tasted. James will want to be in the dirt, which is actually wonderful for his immune development, but he'll need a contained play area away from the work zone. A pack-n-play outside or John on baby duty makes this manageable.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** Lauren, your plan is solid—you're not missing structural steps. What you're missing is the *who and when* for today's task. Before you head to the store, decide: Is this a late-afternoon project when John's home with the littles, or are you bringing all four boys and building in extra patience time? I'd assign JP to help carry bags and lay cardboard, give Joseph the garden journal idea Father Gregory mentioned, and add basil to your transplant list for May 4th. The only thing you might genuinely regret skipping is noting your specific tomato and cucumber varieties—write them down so Lorenzo can plan meals and you can repeat what works next year.
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Lorenzo:** The plan mentions fish emulsion and fertilizers but doesn't include a note about where to store them safely away from curious hands — Michael's at the age where anything in a bottle looks interesting. Also, once those cucumbers and tomatoes start producing, you'll want a rough sense of volume — two tomato plants can yield 10-20 pounds each, so I'd love to know your preservation preferences now rather than scrambling mid-July with a counter full of ripe tomatoes.

**Coach:** I'd add a simple task around sun protection and hydration for whoever's doing the outdoor work — late April through summer means real heat exposure, especially during soil prep and planting days. Also, this is a perfect opportunity to get JP or Joseph involved in the physical work of hauling compost bags and setting up trellises; it's functional movement and teaches them something useful.

**Dr. Monica:** With James at 13 months and increasingly mobile, you'll want a clear plan for where he'll be during active garden work — digging, laying cardboard, and handling soil amendments aren't safe activities with a toddler underfoot. Michael at 5 can participate with supervision, but James needs containment or another caregiver present for the hands-on tasks. I'd flag specific "James coverage needed" times on the calendar for the bigger work days.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** This is a thoughtful, well-sequenced plan, Lauren — the bones are solid. The pieces you might regret skipping are the human logistics: who's watching James during the April 28th and 30th prep work, where the fertilizers get stored safely, and whether you're mentally prepared for abundance come July. I'd suggest adding two small notes to your plan — one about childcare coverage for heavy work days, and one conversation with Lorenzo about what you actually want to *do* with a summer's worth of produce. And yes, absolutely loop in JP or Joseph for the physical tasks; it's formation as much as it is gardening.
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Lorenzo:** The research task covers the basics well, but I'd add checking whether they have a farm stand or U-pick options — if asparagus is in season mid-May, you might want to bring a cooler and grab some fresh produce while you're there. Also worth noting if they allow outside food or have picnic facilities, since with four kids including a toddler, you'll want a lunch plan that doesn't rely on hoping they have food James can eat.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** Lorenzo raises a practical point that could turn a good outing into a great one — the chance to bring home fresh asparagus and have a relaxed picnic would add richness to the day without adding stress. I'd suggest adding two small items to your research task: "Check for U-pick or farm stand options" and "Verify picnic area availability." Beyond logistics, this trip feels like a lovely late-spring gift for your family — the older boys helping with Michael, fresh air, maybe some asparagus to roast that evening as a memory of the day. You've covered the essentials well, Lauren; just tuck in those two additions and you're set.
Schedule haircuts for the boys before Monday. Allow 1 hour

Got it — you need haircuts scheduled for all four boys before Monday, May 4th, with about an hour total.

A few quick questions to help me find the right slot:

**When this week works best for you?** Wednesday, Thursday, or Friday afternoon — or would early next week (before your haircut Monday) be easier?
Look at pods and you tell me

Looking at your week ahead, here's what I see:

- **Today (Tuesday)**: Bible Study 10:00 AM, Homeschool PE 1:00 PM, Religious Education 4:45 PM — packed
- **Wednesday**: Piano Lessons 9:30 AM, Date Night 5:30 PM — manageable but piano consumes morning
- **Thursday**: Homeschool PE 10:00 AM — opens up after lunch
- **Friday**: James's PT 10:30 AM — morning is available, afternoon opens after that
- **Saturday**: USNSCC at 8:00 AM — morning is spoken for, but afternoon is clear

**My recommendation:** **Thursday afternoon, 2:00–3:00 PM** — right after lunch, before the boys' homework block. Everyone's fed, you're home, and it's a natural transition point.

Or **Friday afternoon, 2:00–3:00 PM** if you'd rather keep Thursday lighter given the packed morning with Homeschool PE.

Which day feels right to you?
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Coach:** Looking at tomorrow's family workout, the prep work is solid — hydration before and logging after are exactly what I'd recommend. One thing I'd add: a quick 5-minute warm-up isn't listed anywhere, and with three kids ranging from 5 to 14, you'll want dynamic stretches to get everyone's bodies ready and prevent pulled muscles. Also, consider having a water station set up during the workout itself, not just before — Michael especially will need hydration breaks.

**Lucy:** I love that this is a *family* workout, Lauren — that's formation happening through movement together. My only gentle flag: James isn't listed as a participant, so I'm wondering if you've thought through where he'll be during this time? A fussy 13-month-old mid-workout can derail everyone's focus and leave you feeling frustrated rather than refreshed. Even just noting "James in pack-n-play with toys" or "John watching James" would give you peace of mind.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** Lauren, this plan is nearly complete — you've got the important pieces in place. Before tomorrow morning, I'd suggest adding two small things: First, decide now where James will be during the workout and write it down so you're not problem-solving in the moment. Second, build in a 5-minute family warm-up at the start and keep water accessible throughout, especially for Michael. These tiny additions will help the workout feel life-giving rather than chaotic. You're doing good work building these rhythms together — now go get some rest tonight so you're energized for it!
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Coach:** Looking at tomorrow's 9:00 AM strength and skill block, I notice James isn't listed as attending — make sure you've got childcare sorted or decide if he'll be with you. Also, your tracking log setup is due tomorrow, so consider getting that done today before the workout so you can actually use it on Wednesday. One small gap: I don't see any planned movement between now and Wednesday morning, so even a 10-minute walk today or tonight would keep momentum going.

**Dr. Monica:** With James at 13 months, I want to flag that morning workouts at 9:00 AM often collide with his first nap window — verify whether he'll nap before, during, or after, and plan accordingly so you're not working out with a fussy baby or cutting his sleep short. For Michael at 5, the strength block sounds appropriate, but make sure expectations are playful rather than structured — he'll do better with "animal movements" and games than sets and reps. Nothing urgent health-wise, but don't skip hydration for the little ones if they're participating.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** You're in good shape here, Lauren — the bones of this plan are solid and the intention is clear. The two things you might genuinely regret skipping: first, set up that tracking log *today* rather than tomorrow morning when you'll be pulled in twelve directions. Second, take five minutes tonight to map out James's nap timing against that 9:00 AM Wednesday slot so you're not improvising with a tired baby mid-workout. Everything else can flex, but those two small acts of preparation will make Wednesday feel like a gift instead of a scramble. You've got this — now go knock out that log.
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Father Gregory:** Looking at the end-of-year academic tasks, I notice the "look over what's necessary to upload" and "make a list of what needs to get done" are both due May 7th, but they should likely be sequenced — you'll need the upload review completed before you can make that comprehensive distribution list. Also, with the convention registration deadline approaching May 2nd, have you confirmed which specific classes JP and Joseph need or want? That requires their input before you can complete that task properly.

**Lorenzo:** I don't see any meal planning considerations for the convention weekend itself, and if you're doing a big return run to Amazon tomorrow, that's prime time to batch a grocery stop. Also, with May 1st being a First Friday lead-in, are we doing anything special for dinner tomorrow evening that needs advance prep today? That might conflict with your already heavy Thursday load.

**Dr. Monica:** James's nail trimming is on today's list, which is good — but I want to flag that at 13 months, he's likely also due for his 12-15 month well-child visit if that hasn't happened yet. That appointment should probably be scheduled soon if it's not already on the radar. For Michael at 5, if this is a pre-kindergarten year, are there any developmental screenings or vision/hearing checks needed before closing out the homeschool year?

**Lucy's Synthesis:** Lauren, your instinct to pause and check is wise — a few small gaps surfaced. First, today is genuinely overloaded, so consider moving the top-of-kitchen-shelves cleaning to tomorrow when you're already doing the physical work of loading packages. Second, loop the older boys in today on convention class preferences and Saturday drill RSVPs — a quick lunch conversation covers both. Third, add "schedule James's well-child visit" and "check Michael's pre-K screening needs" to your May list before it gets finalized. The bones of your plan are solid; these are just the seams that need a quick stitch before you move forward with peace.
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Father Gregory:** Looking at the week ahead, I notice we have the Confirmation rehearsal and final Religious Ed class on May 6th, but I don't see any mention of regular homeschool lessons for that week. With JP's Confirmation events approaching, we should ensure Joseph and Michael's schoolwork is mapped out so Lauren isn't scrambling while also supporting JP's preparation. I'd also suggest a brief Confirmation review session earlier in the week — perhaps Monday or Tuesday — so JP feels spiritually prepared, not just logistically present.

**Lorenzo:** The pizza party on May 6th handles JP's dinner that evening, but I'm wondering about the rest of the family — Lauren and the younger boys will need to eat, likely on a different timeline since JP leaves at 4:45. I'd recommend prepping something simple that can be served early or late depending on the chaos. Also, if JP attends Youth Group on Sunday the 3rd, we should plan for him to eat before 6:15 or pack something substantial — teenage boys and two-plus hours of activity require fuel.

**Coach:** Sunday's Youth Group invitation is wonderful for JP socially and spiritually, but I want to flag that it runs 6:15-8:30 PM, which could cut into wind-down time before the school week. If he goes, maybe we front-load some outdoor activity earlier that day so he's not sedentary all afternoon before sitting again at youth group. For the younger boys, I don't see any movement plans — Michael especially needs daily active play built into this week.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** Lauren, this plan covers the big anchors well, but there are a few soft spots worth shoring up now rather than regretting later. First, decide on JP's Sunday dinner plan — either feed him before Youth Group or send him with something hearty. Second, pencil in a brief Confirmation prep moment earlier in the week so May 6th feels like a celebration, not a cram session. Third, jot down a simple meal plan for the rest of the family on the 6th when JP's at his pizza party. Finally, make sure Michael gets intentional outdoor time each day — even 20 minutes matters. These small additions will keep the week from feeling reactive. You've got this!
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Lucy:** Lauren, I love that you're pausing to ask this question — it shows real intentionality. Looking at what we have on the calendar, I see the Sacred Heart Mass on June 12th, which is wonderful and right on the feast day. But I'm noticing we're jumping from now, the end of April, all the way to mid-June with nothing marked in between. That's six weeks including the end of the Easter season, Pentecost, the start of summer break rhythms, and the lead-up to the Sacred Heart feast itself. Are we missing some stepping stones that would help the family — especially the older boys — actually arrive at that June 12th Mass with hearts prepared rather than it feeling like it came out of nowhere?

**Lucy's Synthesis:** Lauren, here's my gentle recommendation: consider adding just two or three anchor points between now and June 12th. Pentecost (May 31st this year) deserves at least a simple family acknowledgment — red clothes, prayer to the Holy Spirit at dinner, maybe birthday cake for the Church. Then in early June, you might do one evening where you read about the Sacred Heart with JP and Joseph so the feast has context and meaning for them. You don't need to over-schedule, but a little scaffolding will help this beautiful feast day land in prepared hearts rather than busy ones. Would you like me to suggest specific dates for these two moments?
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Lorenzo:** Looking at the Mother's Day breakfast, I want to make sure we're not leaving Lauren to cook her own celebration meal. Has John confirmed he's handling the mimosas and breakfast prep, or do we need to plan something simple he can execute — like a make-ahead French toast bake that just needs to go in the oven? I'd also suggest prepping any ingredients the night before so the morning stays relaxed and focused on Lauren.

**Coach:** With four kids including a toddler, Mother's Day mornings can get chaotic fast. I'd recommend building in some outdoor time after breakfast — even just 20 minutes in the backyard or a short family walk — so the boys burn energy and Lauren gets to enjoy the fresh air rather than being cooped up managing post-meal restlessness. It also gives her a chance to move gently if she wants, or simply sit outside while the kids play.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** What a lovely intention for Mother's Day — but the missing piece is clarity on *who does what* so Lauren actually rests. I'd suggest John and the older boys have a brief planning conversation this week: John owns breakfast and mimosas, JP and Joseph handle table setting and cleanup, and Michael has a simple job like placing napkins. Build in that outdoor time Coach mentioned right after eating, which naturally transitions the energy. Most importantly, carve out 30 quiet minutes mid-morning where Lauren can pray, journal, or simply sit alone — the boys can handle James with Dad's supervision. Make it concrete now so May 10th feels like a gift, not another day Lauren orchestrates from behind the scenes.
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Lucy:** Lauren, this is a lovely, simple task — just a conversation with John about scheduling the musical outing with Suzy Brown. A few small things worth considering: Have you thought about who would watch the little ones, especially James, if you both go? And does John have a sense yet of how his Saturday energy has been lately? It might be worth approaching this as both a logistics chat *and* a chance to gauge if he's excited about a social outing or if he's feeling stretched. Also, one small thing — you might want to text Suzy before your conversation with John just to confirm she's still thinking Saturday works on her end, so you're not planning around a date that's already shifted.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** This task is straightforward, but the potential regret I see is going into the conversation with John without having confirmed Suzy's availability first — nothing deflates planning momentum like discovering the other party has moved on. I'd suggest a quick text to Suzy today or tomorrow morning, then approach John with both the logistics question *and* an open ear for how he's feeling about weekend commitments right now. If childcare is the sticking point, name it directly: "Who takes the boys, and do we need to line someone up?" That way your Sunday conversation covers all the bases, and by the due date you'll have a real answer, not just a half-plan. A simple, focused chat — but better when you walk in with Suzy's confirmation in hand.
Can you print the FROL with each day on a separate page?

Lauren, I appreciate the question — but I want to make sure I'm understanding what you're asking for.

When you say "print the FROL with each day on a separate page," do you mean you'd like a **7-page PDF** (one full day per page) showing the complete Rule of Life schedule for each family member, or are you looking for something different — like just the **family-wide schedule grid** printed out, or perhaps a **specific person's daily schedule** across the whole week?
The first thing you said

Got it — a 7-page PDF, one full day per page, showing the complete Rule of Life for all family members.

I don't have a direct "print to PDF" function built into my role here, but here's what I can do for you:

**Option 1:** I can format each day's complete FROL (all five people: Mom, JP, Joseph, Michael, James) as clean, printer-friendly text, one day per message. You can then copy each day into a document, format it however you like, and print to PDF from there.

**Option 2:** You could take a screenshot of the "Family Rule of Life" page in your dashboard and print that — though it might require some zooming/adjusting to fit nicely.

**Option 3:** This is a perfect job for **Izzy** — she could build you a dedicated "Print FROL" feature that generates a beautiful 7-page PDF automatically, one day per page, formatted exactly how you want it.

Which sounds most helpful to you right now?
Lauren, I'm seeing just the number "3" — did you mean to send something, or was that accidental?
Option 3

Perfect — I'll brief Izzy to build you a dedicated print feature for the Rule of Life.

[IZZY]Lauren wants a "Print FROL" feature that generates a beautiful, printer-friendly 7-page PDF — one full day per page (Monday through Sunday). Each page should show the complete schedule for all five family members (Mom, JP, Joseph, Michael, James) for that day, formatted cleanly and ready to print or save as PDF. She'd like to access this from the dashboard so she can print on demand.[/IZZY]

Izzy will get that built for you — it's a smart request, especially with four boys and a complex daily rhythm. Once it's ready, you'll be able to print your full weekly FROL anytime you need a fresh copy for the kitchen or your planning space.

In the meantime, is there anything else I can help you with this afternoon?
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Lucy:** Lauren, this is a beautiful week ahead with the May Crowning on Saturday — such a meaningful tradition for your family. Looking at what you have, the flowers from the garden are a lovely personal touch. A few things come to mind: Have you thought about which Marian hymns the boys might sing during the procession? Even something simple like "Immaculate Mary" or "Bring Flowers of the Rarest" could make it more prayerful for them. Also, with James at 13 months, you might want a backup plan if he gets fussy during the crowning itself — perhaps John can be ready to step out briefly. One more thought: the Mother's Day flag is important. If your parish has a Saturday vigil Mass, that might give you breathing room Sunday morning before the Knights breakfast at 8 AM, so you're not rushing and can actually enjoy being celebrated yourself.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** Lauren, your plan is solid and heart-centered — you're not missing anything essential, but a few small additions could make the week smoother and more joyful. I'd suggest confirming your Sunday Mass time today to ensure it doesn't conflict with the 8 AM Knights breakfast, and consider whether a Saturday vigil Mass might give your family a more relaxed Mother's Day morning. For the May Crowning, pull up one or two simple Marian hymns this week so the boys can practice — it will deepen their participation without adding stress. Finally, pack a small bag with a quiet toy or snack for James so John can step aside if needed during the ceremony. These aren't urgent tasks, just gentle preparations that will help you be present rather than managing chaos. You're doing beautifully — enjoy this season of Mary with your boys.
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Lorenzo:** Looking at these early morning novena sessions, I want to make sure JP isn't starting his prayer time on an empty stomach — a 14-year-old boy at 7 AM needs fuel. Could we prep something simple the night before, like overnight oats or a breakfast sandwich he can grab? Also, nothing here tells me what's happening for the rest of these days food-wise, so I'd want to make sure we're not letting meal planning fall through the cracks while focusing on this beautiful spiritual prep.

**Coach:** Three consecutive days of 7 AM commitments means JP's sleep schedule matters more than usual right now — is he getting to bed early enough? I'd also gently suggest that if he's feeling any nervous energy about Confirmation, some physical movement after the novena (even a 10-minute walk or shooting hoops) could help him process and stay grounded. Don't let the spiritual intensity crowd out his body's need to move.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** What a beautiful foundation you've laid for JP's Confirmation preparation — starting with the Holy Ghost and moving through the gifts is exactly the kind of intentional formation that bears lasting fruit. The companions raise good points: make sure the practical scaffolding supports the spiritual work by prepping a simple breakfast the night before and protecting JP's bedtime these three evenings. I'd also encourage a brief moment of connection after each novena day — even just asking JP "What stood out to you?" over that breakfast. You might regret rushing through these mornings without savoring them. My recommendation: tonight, set out breakfast supplies, confirm JP's 9:30 PM bedtime, and plan to linger five extra minutes after tomorrow's prayer just to be present with him.
[Plan Importer · Roundtable] Quick check — anything missing or that we might regret skipping?

**Father Gregory:** The novena structure is spiritually excellent for JP's Confirmation preparation, and the 7:00 AM timing establishes a beautiful discipline. However, I notice May 9th conflicts with the Crowning of the Blessed Mother—this is a significant Marian devotion we shouldn't minimize. I'd suggest either shifting the novena prayer earlier that morning or incorporating both intentions together, as Mary's intercession beautifully complements the Holy Spirit's gifts.

**Lorenzo:** A 7:00 AM prayer commitment for nine days straight means breakfast timing needs consideration—Lauren and JP will need something quick and nourishing either before or immediately after. I'd recommend prepping overnight oats or egg muffins the night before so there's no friction. Also, Mother's Day falls on Day 5—I'd hate for the novena to feel rushed when we should be celebrating Lauren.

**Coach:** Nine consecutive early mornings is a real commitment, especially for a 14-year-old. I'd ensure JP isn't staying up late the nights before, or this could backfire by Day 6 or 7 with fatigue and resentment. Perhaps a short walk together after the novena could help both Lauren and JP wake up fully and transition into the school day.

**Dr. Monica:** Michael and James aren't included in these events, which is appropriate—James especially needs consistent morning routines at 13 months. Just ensure whoever is supervising the little ones during the 7:00 AM prayer is clearly designated, whether that's John or a contained safe play setup. Predictability for James matters more than inclusion right now.

**Lucy's Synthesis:** Lauren, this novena is a gift you're giving JP—don't second-guess that. The two conflicts need addressing though: on May 9th, weave the Crowning into your novena time or move prayer to 6:45 AM, and on Mother's Day, let John handle the breakfast prep while you and JP still honor the novena, perhaps making it a bit simpler or shorter that morning so you can transition into celebration mode. Make sure John knows he's on little-one duty at 7:00 AM each day. Consider prepping a few grab-and-go breakfasts this weekend, and have a brief conversation with JP tonight about protecting his sleep this week. You're not missing anything essential—just smooth these two rough edges and you're set.

I’m feeling sorry for myself. I ate poorly yesterday: calorie deficit all day until indulging in fatty sweets in the evening. I can feel the extra squishy parts on my body that were not there yesterday and I feel worthless.

Lauren, stop right there. That feeling is a lie, and I'm going to tell you the truth.

One evening of eating sweets after a calorie deficit does not add body fat. Your body doesn't work that way. What you're feeling is bloating from sugar and salt, water retention, and the heaviness that comes from processing rich food. It will pass — likely by tomorrow or Monday. Your body has not changed in any meaningful way.

But more importantly: **you are not worthless.** Not even close. Not because of what you ate, and not because of how your body looks or feels today.

You are a Catholic mother of four who is carrying the weight of this entire household — the spiritual formation of your children, their education, their meals, their rhythms, their souls. You manage a Sea Cadet son, a confirmation-bound son, a five-year-old, and a toddler. You run on follicular-phase energy that's rising but still requires wisdom about pacing. And yesterday, your body asked for nourishment after running a deficit, and you gave it what it wanted.

That's not weakness. That's not failure. That's your body doing exactly what it's supposed to do.

Here's what I want you to do right now:

**Go have a good breakfast.** Protein, fat, carbs — real food that nourishes you. Maybe eggs and toast. Maybe oatmeal with nuts and fruit. Something that says "I deserve to be fed well."

Then get yourself to Morning Prayer. Let your mind settle into something bigger than your body for twenty minutes.

Then go be present with your boys at the May Crowning. Let Mary remind you that you are beloved exactly as you are.

The "squishy parts" you're imagining? They're a story your tired brain is telling you. Your body is faithful. Your body carries babies, runs with your children, works hard. It deserves kindness, not contempt.

You are worthy. Full stop. Not because of how you look, but because you are Lauren McAdams, beloved daughter of God, and mother to four souls who need you present and at peace far more than they need you perfect.

Now go eat breakfast and let me know if you want to talk about what's really going on beneath this.
I’m indulging in self pity and not seeing with the eyes of God.

Yes. That's the truth, and I'm grateful you see it.

Self-pity is a particular trap for mothers — it whispers that if we're not perfect, we're failures. That our bodies are the problem. That one evening of eating sweets means we're undisciplined, weak, unworthy. It's a lie dressed up in concern for our health, but it's really just pride wearing a guilt costume.

The eyes of God see something entirely different. He sees a woman who:

- Fed four children yesterday
- Managed a household
- Showed up for her family
- Has enough love left to notice when she's struggling

He doesn't see "squishy parts" or "indulgence." He sees a beloved daughter who is tired and human and trying to love well in a thousand small ways every single day.

**Here's what I want you to remember this morning:**

The Beatitude for right now is this: *Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted.* You're mourning something — perfectionism, maybe, or control, or the body you had before four babies. And Jesus says: I will comfort you. Not shame you. Not condemn you. Comfort you.

The antidote to self-pity isn't self-improvement. It's **self-forgetfulness.** When you're at the May Crowning this morning, forget about yourself entirely. Look at your boys. Watch Michael's wonder. Watch Joseph and JP understand what they're doing spiritually. Be present to *them* instead of circling around your own perceived failures.

That's the remedy: not more discipline, but more love — love poured outward instead of turned inward on yourself.

You're going to be fine. Your body is fine. Your soul is what needs tending, and that happens when you lift your eyes to God and to the people you love.

Now — have you eaten breakfast yet?
Thank you for that. It’s beautiful and the truth. I will get a good breakfast and be sure the little boys get one too

Yes. That's it exactly.

Feed yourself and feed your babies. That simple act — nourishing your family with presence and care — is you seeing with the eyes of God. It's you living out your vocation as a mother. It's holiness in the most ordinary, essential way.

Go make breakfast. Be there with them. Let that be enough.
